articles.title: TODAY, COMPLIANCE IS YOUR SHORTEST RELATIONSHIP
articles.summary: We meet. We click. In 15 hours, you're SOC 2 compliant.

articles.title: WHY THIEL FELLOWS WIN
articles.summary: The Thiel Fellowship pays young talents $100,000 to leave college and start companies, challenging the traditional education bubble. It prizes creativity and individuality over conventional metrics like GPA, producing successful companies like Figma and Ethereum. Originating from ideas by Peter Thiel and colleagues, the fellowship has inspired a new venture fund, 1517 Fund, that supports young entrepreneurs.

articles.title: MARTIN CASADO ISN'T BACKING DOWN. YOU CAN'T IGNORE THE VC CONSENSUS
articles.summary: Non-consensual investing is risky early on, as successful startups usually attract competition and higher valuations. Access and strategic fund structures are key in venture capital. There is a defense-tech boom happening in Silicon Valley driven by changing warfare dynamics and new opportunities in selling to the Pentagon.

articles.title: META'S AI HIRING SPREE BACKFIRES AS STAR RECRUITS EXIT WITHIN WEEKS
articles.summary: ChatGPT co-creator Shengjia Zhao threatened to quit Meta within days of joining and signed paperwork to return to OpenAI before being appeased with the “chief AI scientist” title. Other high-profile hires, including former OpenAI researchers Avi Verma and Ethan Knight, left after less than a month.

articles.title: DON'T LET CULTURE DRIFT AS YOU SCALE
articles.summary: Culture changes every time you hire someone new, but most founders only notice when it's already broken - usually around employee 20 when proximity to founders starts fading. The key isn't just defining values but turning them into visible behaviors - celebrate when someone lives them, call out when someone doesn't - because as the military says, doing something below standard and saying nothing sets a new standard. Culture drifts about every six months during rapid growth, so plan regular resets at off-sites where you reconnect on what values look like in practice.

articles.title: THE $0.20/DAY AI SYSTEM THAT CONVERTS TRIAL USERS INTO PAYING CUSTOMERS
articles.summary: Podscan uses generative AI to convert trial users into paying customers by implementing an LLM scoring system six hours after sign-up, analyzing user behavior and attributes, and sending personalized follow-up emails. It uses an automated onboarding process to generate customized alert keywords based on user classification and project descriptions. Podscan's AI-assisted dashboard allows users to create alerts, optimizing engagement and onboarding while maintaining cost-efficiency.

articles.title: THE AI VS. JUNIOR TALENT DILEMMA
articles.summary: AI is increasingly replacing junior roles, giving rise to strategic risks for companies. A lack of young talent can hinder leadership pipeline development and disconnect businesses from emerging cultural trends. Eliminating juniors also forfeits potential AI advancements driven by their creativity and adaptability.

articles.title: RIVETER
articles.summary: Thousands of AI search agents running in parallel.

articles.title: FLOUNDER MODE
articles.summary: Kevin Kelly has built clocks that tick for 10,000 years, co-founded WIRED, wrote the "1,000 true fans" essay that launched the creator economy, and advised Spielberg on Minority Report - but he's never started a company and doesn't want to. He works "Hollywood style," jumping between projects that interest him, which Silicon Valley sees as unserious compared to grinding toward unicorn status. While tech culture fetishizes founders who get scurvy from eating only ramen or "torture people into greatness," Kelly just seems happy, following interests not for money or dominance but because the well of curiosity is bottomless. The real question isn't whether you can be great without suffering - it's why we insist greatness requires misery.

articles.title: HOW COSTCO WON IN JAPAN
articles.summary: People waited eight hours to enter Costco's new Okinawa store - five to park, three more to get inside - in a country where Walmart, Tesco, and Carrefour all failed and fled. Costco succeeded by adapting everything: building up instead of out with escalator ramps for carts because land costs too much, selling 30% fresh food, including restaurant-quality sushi at $20 for 48 pieces, and paying 60% above minimum wage, which forced local shops to match or close. The real genius was understanding Japanese shopping culture - customers split bulk purchases with friends and neighbors, giving Costco 162,000 members per warehouse versus 112,000 in America, plus it let corner stores resell Kirkland products during COVID at 20% markups because spreading the brand mattered more than controlling distribution when membership fees generate half your profit.

articles.title: SURVIVING COVID, PMMC FIT, & SCALING TO $200M ARR
articles.summary: Product-market fit isn't enough - you need PMMC fit, which means having a product, market, profitable model, and channels that actually work. Super.com nearly died during COVID when refunds exceeded bookings. It burned money on TikTok ads, assuming viral content drove bookings, but people searching "hotel tonight" use Google, not social media. The same blindness almost killed its product - it thought lower-income households wanted cheaper vacations. After making 200 employees cash paychecks at payday lenders and shops at Walmart, it learned these customers needed everyday financial help, not travel deals. It pivoted from discount hotels to a membership program for credit building, grocery cashback, and basic financial services - now it's at $200 million revenue.

articles.title: THE FIGHT TO OWN WEB AGENT IDENTITY
articles.summary: The internet's biggest traffic cop can't identify which AI agent is which or why they're there.

articles.title: FACING FEAR
articles.summary: Every company has a moment where the CEO realizes they're completely screwed and has to tell investors and employees who definitely don't want to hear it.

articles.title: ASSORT HEALTH NABS $50M TO AUTOMATE PATIENT PHONE CALLS, SOURCES SAY
articles.summary: Assort Health raised $50 million in a Series B funding round led by Lightspeed Venture Partners to automate patient communication in specialty healthcare.
